caption: Revenue-recognition illustration showing how a $25 Robux purchase becomes $3 of first-month revenue and $22 of deferred revenue over a 23-month payer life.
Management's four-bucket cost framework separates cost of revenue, DevEx, non-SBC personnel, and selected infrastructure and trust-and-safety expense.
DAU history by geography and age shows the early post-pandemic shift toward older and international users.
Hours-engaged history by geography and age shows how engagement growth broadened beyond the original under-13 U.S. cohort.
Quarterly revenue-to-bookings bridge from 2019 through Q2 2022 captures the pandemic surge and subsequent bookings normalization.
Quarterly adjusted-EBITDA reconciliation from 2019 through Q2 2022 shows profitability weakening as investment resumed after the pandemic surge.
Operating-metric definitions establish new payers, returning payers, monthly repurchase rate, and bookings per monthly payer as distinct retention and monetization diagnostics.
DevEx history shows creator payouts rising as a share of bookings, clarifying how platform growth is shared with creators.
Non-SBC personnel cost, headcount, and cost ratios through Q2 2023 provide the baseline for later labor operating leverage.
DAUs by geography and age show post-pandemic growth continuing across cohorts rather than depending on one region.
Bookings by geography show international expansion while preserving the large U.S. and Canada monetization base.
Payer chart separates new and returning accounts, repurchase rate, and spend per payer across 13 quarters, exposing retention and mix separately.
Basic and potentially dilutive share counts through Q2 2023 show the size and composition of the early post-listing equity overhang.
DAU growth by geography and age shows broad acceleration into Q2 2025, including faster international and older-user expansion.
Regional bookings history shows viral growth translating into monetization across every geography, not only the largest market.
The last detailed payer-cohort chart reports new versus returning payers and repurchase rate through Q2 2025 before those diagnostics disappeared.
Selected infrastructure and trust-and-safety expense declined from 17% to 11% of bookings between Q2 2023 and Q2 2025 despite rapid usage growth.
Share table shows 7% basic-share growth but only 2% growth in total outstanding and potentially dilutive shares in Q2 2025.
Q3 2025 summary captures the viral-growth peak: DAUs rose 70%, hours 91%, and bookings 70% year over year.
DevEx chart shows creator payouts increasing faster than bookings during the viral-growth period and limiting platform capture.
Selected infrastructure and trust-and-safety cost ratios remain contained as engagement nearly doubled, evidence of technical scale leverage.
Q4 2025 guidance anticipated a $325 million capex step-up and only $100-$130 million of free cash flow despite strong bookings growth.
Regional DAU growth remained very high in Q4 2025 while Roblox paused age reporting during the transition from self-reported to age-checked data.
Quarterly free-cash-flow history shows Q4 2025 capex reaching $298 million, confirming the planned GPU and data-center investment step-up.
Initial FY2026 guidance called for 22%-26% bookings growth, $470-$520 million of capex, and $1.60-$1.82 billion of free cash flow.
The payer chart now reports only aggregate monthly payers and spend per payer, removing the prior new-versus-returning split and repurchase rate.
DevEx reached 24% of bookings in Q1 2026, showing creator payouts absorbing a larger share of platform demand.
Selected infrastructure and trust-and-safety expense remained near 11% of bookings in Q1 2026 despite slower growth.
Updated FY2026 guidance reduced bookings growth to 8%-12% and free cash flow to $1.05-$1.28 billion while leaving capex unchanged.
Metric methodology disclosure warns that age-checked data are not comparable with historical self-reported age cohorts.
